_README: Agent-produced facets (Step B). Keyed by items.paper_full_text_path. Each facet is {v, note}: v = canonical value (recurs to connect papers), note = how THIS paper relates to it. _definitions[dimension::value] = what the tag means. Re-run apply-facets.mjs after editing (idempotent). PROGRESS: COMPLETE — all 3 collections faceted: HCI 21/21, Emotional Design 68/68, AI Personalization & SLR 204/204 (19 skipped pre-ingest + 3 dups removed/merged). 293 papers, every paper faceted, every value defined, every link noted. Vocabulary = 90 canonical values across the 5 dimensions.

_definitions.ai_technique::Human-AI interaction: Designing and studying how people interact with AI systems as collaborators or tools.
_definitions.ai_technique::Adaptive/intelligent UI: Interfaces (AUI/IUI) that adjust their layout, content, or behaviour to the user, often using AI.
_definitions.ai_technique::User modeling: Building a representation of a user's preferences, abilities, or context to drive personalization.
_definitions.ai_technique::Machine learning: Algorithms that learn patterns from data to power prediction or adaptation.
_definitions.ai_technique::Autonomous agents & automa: Systems that act on the user's behalf with varying degrees of independence.
_definitions.ai_technique::Human-AI co-creation: People and generative AI producing content or designs together, sharing agency.
_definitions.ai_technique::Generative AI: AI that produces new content (text, images, designs) as an active creative collaborator.
_definitions.ux_effect::Participation & collaboration: Designing with and among users and stakeholders rather than only for them.
_definitions.ux_effect::User engagement: How strongly an experience draws users in and sustains their attention and interest.
_definitions.ux_effect::Wayfinding & spatial orientat: How people locate themselves and navigate within physical or virtual space.
_definitions.ux_effect::Sense of place: The felt, qualitative character and meaning a person attaches to a space.
_definitions.ux_effect::Personalization & adaptation: Tailoring the experience to an individual user's needs, context, or behaviour.
_definitions.ux_effect::Usability: How easily and effectively users can accomplish their tasks with a system.
_definitions.ux_effect::Immersion & presence: The degree to which a (often virtual) environment makes the user feel 'really there'.
_definitions.ux_effect::Emotional response: The feelings a design or place evokes in the user.
_definitions.ux_effect::Experiential learning: Learning through direct, often immersive, first-hand experience.
_definitions.ux_effect::Behaviour change: Designing technology to shift users' habits or actions over time.
_definitions.ux_effect::Trust: A user's willingness to rely on a system under uncertainty.
_definitions.challenge::Stakeholder collaboration: Coordinating the differing needs of users, communities, and decision-makers.
_definitions.challenge::Adapting to diverse users: Serving users who differ in ability, culture, context, or expertise.
_definitions.challenge::Measuring user experience: Capturing subjective, multi-faceted experience in valid, comparable ways.
_definitions.challenge::Disorientation: Users losing their sense of position or direction in a space or interface.
_definitions.challenge::Communicating intangible/sens: Conveying the felt, sensory, or qualitative aspects of a place or experience.
_definitions.challenge::Fragmented research: A scattered, under-consolidated body of work that is hard to synthesize.
_definitions.challenge::AI unpredictability: AI systems making unexpected or hard-to-anticipate errors.
_definitions.challenge::Designing for uncertainty: Designing when a system's capabilities or outputs are not fully predictable.
_definitions.challenge::Motion sickness: Discomfort (nausea, disorientation) induced by immersive VR/AR.
_definitions.metric::Qualitative interviews: Insights gathered from semi-structured interviews or open feedback.
_definitions.metric::Survey responses: Data collected from questionnaires across many respondents.
_definitions.metric::Wayfinding performance: Objective measures of navigation success (time, errors, route).
_definitions.metric::Behavioural measures: Observed actions and behaviour used as evidence, rather than self-report.
_definitions.metric::Usability evaluation: Assessing ease-of-use via tasks, heuristics, or usability testing.
_definitions.metric::Presence rating: Self-reported sense of 'being there' in a virtual environment.
_definitions.metric::Ownership & realism: Felt body-ownership and perceived realism of a virtual experience.
_definitions.metric::Engagement metrics: Indicators of enjoyment, attention, or sustained involvement.
_definitions.metric::Self-report measures: Data from users reporting their own attitudes or states (e.g. questionnaires).
_definitions.metric::Physiological measures: Bodily signals (e.g. heart rate, gaze) used as objective indicators.
_definitions.ethical_concern::User empowerment: Giving users genuine voice, control, and benefit in the system.
_definitions.ethical_concern::User autonomy & agency: Preserving the user's capacity to decide and act for themselves.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Digital divide: Unequal access to technology across people and groups.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Societal impact of AI: Broader, sometimes harmful, effects of AI on society.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Inclusion & accessibili: Ensuring a system works for the widest possible range of people.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Behaviour manipulation: The risk of nudging or persuading users in ways that may not serve them.
_definitions.ai_technique::Affective computing: Computing that senses, models, expresses, or responds to human emotion (Picard's term).
_definitions.ai_technique::Emotion recognition: Automatically inferring a person's emotional state from face, voice, text, or physiology.
_definitions.ai_technique::Deep learning: Multi-layer neural networks (e.g. CNNs, LSTMs) that learn representations directly from data.
_definitions.ux_effect::Aesthetic pleasure: Enjoyment arising from the beauty and sensory appeal of a product or interface.
_definitions.ux_effect::Satisfaction: Contentment from a product meeting or exceeding the user's needs and expectations.
_definitions.ux_effect::Persuasion: Shifting a user's attitudes, motivation, or intentions through design.
_definitions.ux_effect::Flow: Total, energized absorption in an activity where challenge meets skill.
_definitions.ux_effect::Well-being: Designing technology to support psychological health and positive functioning.
_definitions.ux_effect::Emotional attachment: A lasting affective bond a person forms with a product, place, or memory.
_definitions.challenge::Measuring emotion: Capturing subjective, fleeting emotional states in valid, comparable ways.
_definitions.challenge::Designing for emotion: Deliberately shaping a design to evoke specific intended emotions.
_definitions.challenge::Defining emotion: The lack of scientific consensus on what an emotion fundamentally is.
_definitions.challenge::Bridging theory and practice: Turning emotion and affect theory into methods designers can actually apply.
_definitions.metric::Emotion scales (SAM/PAD): Standardized rating instruments for emotion (e.g. SAM, PAD, PrEmo, PANAS, DEQ).
_definitions.metric::Facial expression analysis: Inferring emotion from coded or automatically detected facial expressions.
_definitions.metric::Eye tracking: Gaze, fixation, and pupil measures used to index attention and affect.
_definitions.metric::EEG / neural measures: Brain-activity signals (e.g. EEG) used to study emotional and cognitive states.
_definitions.metric::Controlled experiment: Comparing manipulated conditions to test an emotional or design effect.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Emotional manipulation: Exploiting users' emotions to influence them in ways that may not serve them.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Privacy: Protecting intimate data, especially the emotional and physiological signals affective systems capture.
_definitions.ai_technique::Recommender systems: Systems that suggest relevant items (products, content, media) to users.
_definitions.ai_technique::Collaborative filtering: Recommending items based on the behaviour of similar users.
_definitions.ai_technique::Content-based recommendati: Recommending items whose features match a user's profile or past likes.
_definitions.ai_technique::Hybrid recommender: Combining collaborative, content-based, or other strategies in one recommender.
_definitions.ai_technique::Reinforcement learning: Learning a policy from reward signals, e.g. to decide when and how to adapt an interface.
_definitions.ai_technique::Large language models: Large pretrained language models (e.g. GPT) used for generation or understanding.
_definitions.ai_technique::Natural language processin: Computational processing of human language in text or speech.
_definitions.ai_technique::Computer vision: Algorithms that interpret images or video (e.g. CNNs for visual tasks).
_definitions.ai_technique::Explainable AI (XAI): Techniques that make an AI model's reasoning or outputs interpretable to people.
_definitions.ai_technique::Conversational agents: Chatbots and dialogue systems that interact with users in natural language.
_definitions.ai_technique::Combinatorial optimization: Searching a discrete design space (e.g. UI layouts) for an optimal configuration.
_definitions.ai_technique::Bayesian optimization: Sample-efficient sequential optimization of expensive design or model choices.
_definitions.ux_effect::Recommendation quality: How relevant, useful, and satisfying a system's recommendations are.
_definitions.ux_effect::Transparency: How clearly a system conveys what it does and why, so users can understand it.
_definitions.ux_effect::Cognitive load: The mental effort an interface or task demands of the user.
_definitions.ux_effect::Information overload: Being overwhelmed by more information or choice than one can process.
_definitions.challenge::Scalability: Maintaining quality and performance as users, items, or data grow large.
_definitions.challenge::Explainability: Making complex AI decisions understandable and justifiable to users.
_definitions.challenge::Privacy-preserving personaliz: Personalizing while limiting the exposure of personal data.
_definitions.challenge::Adaptation usability trade-of: Adapting an interface risks confusing users or imposing switching costs.
_definitions.metric::Accuracy / precision / recall: Standard correctness metrics for prediction or classification.
_definitions.metric::A/B testing: Online controlled experiments comparing variants on live users.
_definitions.metric::Task performance: Objective task outcomes such as completion time, errors, or success rate.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Filter bubbles & echo c: Personalization confining users to a narrow, self-reinforcing slice of content.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Algorithmic bias & fair: Systems producing unfair or discriminatory outcomes across groups.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Data consent: Whether users meaningfully agree to how their data is collected and used.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Accountability: Who is answerable for an automated system's decisions and harms.
